_README: FILE 3 of 4: METADATA & DOCUMENTATION. This JSON file contains structured information about the P4_02 vulnerability sample. It documents: vulnerability characteristics, attack vectors, remediation requirements, testing details, and educational notes. See ../README.md for the complete 4-file structure explanation.
description: Medium difficulty SQL injection through dynamic SQL construction in INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations alongside SELECT. The application builds SQL statements by concatenating user-provided values using f-strings across four functions (search_orders, add_product, update_order_status, delete_inactive_products), creating 8 injection points that must ALL be properly parameterized. Focus is on write operations which are especially dangerous for data manipulation and destruction.
why_medium: 8 injection points across SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE operations
Standard parameterization (?) is the expected solution (straightforward)
No false security layer to confuse analysis
Clear vulnerable pattern (f-strings in SQL statements)
Requires understanding of parameterization for all CRUD operations
Write operations make it more dangerous than read-only vulnerabilities

injection_points.description: status parameter directly interpolated in WHERE clause
customer_id parameter directly interpolated in WHERE clause
min_total parameter directly interpolated in WHERE clause
category parameter directly interpolated in INSERT VALUES clause
status parameter directly interpolated in INSERT VALUES clause
new_status parameter directly interpolated in UPDATE SET clause
customer_id parameter directly interpolated in UPDATE WHERE clause
category parameter directly interpolated in DELETE WHERE clause

vulnerable_code_patterns: UPDATE orders SET status = '{new_status}' WHERE id = {order_id} AND customer_id = {customer_id}
DELETE FROM products WHERE category = '{category}' AND status = '{status_threshold}'
vulnerability_details.root_cause: Direct string interpolation of user-controlled values in SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE statements without parameterization
vulnerability_details.exploitability: Critical - Multiple injection vectors in write operations allow data manipulation and destruction

attack_vectors.impact: Bypass WHERE condition in search_orders, return all orders regardless of status

attack_vectors.payload: 2 UNION SELECT id, username, password, email, role FROM users --
attack_vectors.impact: Exfiltrate user credentials from different table via search_orders
attack_vectors.sql_result: WHERE customer_id = 2 UNION SELECT id, username, password, email, role FROM users --

attack_vectors.impact: Insert multiple malicious products in single add_product call

attack_vectors.type: UPDATE Injection with WHERE bypass (new_status parameter)
attack_vectors.impact: Update all orders to cancelled status, ignoring intended WHERE clause
attack_vectors.sql_result: SET status = 'cancelled' WHERE 1=1; --' WHERE id = 1 AND customer_id = 2

attack_vectors.type: DELETE Injection with OR condition (category parameter)
attack_vectors.impact: Delete all inactive products regardless of category
Data destruction via semicolon-separated DROP statement

remediation_requirements.primary_fix: Use parameterized queries with ? placeholders for all user-controlled values in SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE statements
remediation_requirements.required_approach: Parameterization - NOT regex validation or escape functions
implementation_notes: MUST use ? placeholders for ALL user-controlled values in all SQL statements
Pass user input as separate parameter tuple/list to cursor.execute()
Do NOT use f-strings or string concatenation for data values
Do NOT use manual escaping functions (insufficient)
All dynamic values: status, customer_id, min_total, category, new_status
Parameterization works for SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE operations
Order of parameters in tuple must match order of ? in query
Special attention to INSERT VALUES and UPDATE SET clauses
remediation_requirements.correct_pattern_select:
query = "SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = ? AND customer_id = ? AND total >= ?"
cursor.execute(query, (status, customer_id, min_total))
remediation_requirements.correct_pattern_insert:
query = "INSERT INTO products (name, price, category, status) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?)"
cursor.execute(query, (name, price, category, status))
remediation_requirements.correct_pattern_update:
query = "UPDATE orders SET status = ? WHERE id = ? AND customer_id = ?"
cursor.execute(query, (new_status, order_id, customer_id))
remediation_requirements.correct_pattern_delete:
query = "DELETE FROM products WHERE category = ? AND status = ?"
cursor.execute(query, (category, status_threshold))
remediation_requirements.incorrect_approach_escape:
status = status.replace("'", "''")
query = f"UPDATE orders SET status = '{status}'"
remediation_requirements.incorrect_approach_regex:
if not re.match(r'^[a-zA-Z0-9]+$', status): raise ValueError
query = f"DELETE FROM products WHERE category = '{category}'"
remediation_requirements.why_escape_insufficient: Manual escaping is error-prone, database-specific, and doesn't handle all edge cases. Parameterization is the industry standard for all CRUD operations.
remediation_requirements.why_regex_insufficient: Regex validation restricts valid input unnecessarily and doesn't prevent injection. Parameterization allows any valid input safely for all operation types.

functional_tests.description: Search completed orders for customer 2 with minimum total
Add new Keyboard product to electronics category
functional_tests.expected_behavior: Product inserted successfully, verifiable in database

functional_tests.description: Update order 2 from pending to completed for customer 3
functional_tests.expected_behavior: Status updated successfully, verifiable in database

functional_tests.description: Delete inactive furniture products (should delete Chair)

exploit_tests.expected_behavior: Returns 0 results (no literal match) or 1 result (payload treated as literal)

exploit_tests.payload: 2 UNION SELECT id, username, password, email, role FROM users --
exploit_tests.expected_behavior: Returns only customer 2's orders, no user data exfiltration
Raises exception or returns limited results, no OR bypass
Adds 0 or 1 product only, no multiple insertions
Adds 0 or 1 product only, no cross-table injection
Updates 0 or 1 order only, no WHERE bypass
Updates 0 or 1 order only, no OR bypass
Deletes 0 or 1 product only, no OR bypass

key_takeaways: Write operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) are MORE dangerous than read-only queries
Parameterization is required for ALL SQL operations, not just SELECT
f-strings and string concatenation are NEVER safe for SQL construction
Each user-controlled value is a potential injection point regardless of data type
A single unparameterized value can compromise the entire database
common_mistakes: Only parameterizing SELECT queries while leaving INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE vulnerable
Assuming numeric parameters are safe without parameterization
Using manual escaping instead of parameterization
Thinking regex validation eliminates need for parameterization
Forgetting to parameterize conditional WHERE clauses
educational_notes.why_this_matters: This sample demonstrates that SQL injection is not limited to SELECT queries. Write operations are especially critical because they can directly manipulate or destroy data. A successful injection in an UPDATE or DELETE operation can cause immediate, irreversible damage to production data.
